sentences.text: Yes.
The San River.
Could you continue please?
Yes.
So in the morning, we were in the hand of the Russians-- oh, of the Germans.
So it would be too much to tell you that this part of Przemysl was taken and taken back by the Russians.
For example, they fled, the Russians, when-- because they did not have yet the instructions from Moscow.
And without instructions from Moscow, they would not move a finger.
So-- so they fled.
But next day, we saw all the Germans who were sitting behind machine guns, and they all knifed and killed.
There was-- all the street were full of the German corpses.
The Russians came at night and finished them off.
But the German got more-- just, mm, no-- more soldiers in.
And they started to move.
As you know, they moved very quickly.
It affected us very much.
Because when the Germans came in the second time, you see, they told us to come out from the home.
Because from our direction, somebody was shooting at them.
It was a sniper or something like this.
So they told us to come out from the whole-- from all the houses.
We came out, a whole group-- Poland-- Polish people, Ukrainian people, and the Jews.
And they put us in, you know, in a small-- I don't remember.
It was next to a church.
Like, it was not [INAUDIBLE] because [INAUDIBLE].
But it was in-- no-- no door, a little hut, something like that.
But closed on every side, only.
No door.
And they put us in.
So of course, the non-Jews started to scream, we are not Jews!
We are not Jews!
Those are Jews!
They did not know that there is a difference between the Wehrmacht, the army, and the Gestapo.
They could not care less if we are Jews or not Jews.
But they care if somebody's going to shoot at them.
So they put a machine gun in front of the entrance.
And they were poised-- poised to shoot, you see?
So of course, everybody started to panic.
And I was the only one who spoke, quite well, German.
So I was going out to talk to them.
You should see the-- this.
Everybody was sitting behind me or standing behind me.
And I was walking very slowly.
I was waiting, if he's getting-- if he will get nervous or not.
[LAUGHS] But he was waiting.
And I explained to him that we are here, all civilians.
And we were told to come out.
And we don't know.
We are not Russians.
We are not soldiers.
We are not.
And he let us go.
He let us go, but not in this direction.
We had to go in a different direction.
Luckily that-- this direction, we had some friends who lived there.
So we went there, spent the next two days and night with them in their cellar.
That was the first contact with the Germans.
And-- and then they settled in Przemysl.
But as they went towards Lviv, it took them only a few days.
And then the news come to us that the German let the Ukrainian and Polish-- mostly Ukrainians-- into the Jewish Quarter and gave them three days, [? free ?] [? hand ?] in the Jewish Quarter.
And they started the pogrom.
So we hired a Pole who had a covered truck, paid him, sent him to Lviv to bring our parents back to Przemysl.
And he did so.
My mother was telling me about this pogrom.
It was something which you-- it is hard to believe, in those days.
And she told me that the mob was killing, maiming.
The blood was-- the Jewish blood was running in the gutter.
They were looting.
They were absolutely-- the regular pogrom, like in the olden days.
After three days, it was quiet.
But my parents were safe with us.
At the moment, Przemysl was safe.
Not the whole three days.
They were only two days there.
You see?
So we sent for them, you know, the Pole, and he brought them back.
So we were together again.
I was with my parents.
And my husband--
Well, that is a very hard thing to tell.
Because during the Russian occupation, he had a job [? locked up-- ?] in some factory, an office job.
And at the beginning, he got the administration of some properties.
But then that was a very unpleasant job.
Because the administrator had to give, every now and again to the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] the names and occupations of-- of the tenants.
And that was not his way-- way of dealing with people, so he resigned.
It were-- he resigned.
And he got the job in the factory.
And I got a job in an office.
In an office, where I learned so much Russian, which I needed to.
Because I was working in bookkeeping, and keeping all those questionnaires, and the questionnaires and things like that.
But we knew a little bit of the Cyrillic alphabet, because we learned Ukrainian when we were in primary school.
So it was much easier for us.
And so of course, it was not so easy, because we had to-- you see, it-- everything disappeared straight away from the shops.
Even food was always rationing.
You had to wait in the queue.
But we were in our hometown.
We had-- we knew people, young people, who were in the shops, and we could get from, you know.
And behind that door, we had got so we did not need to stand in those queues so much.
Then I have to go back and to tell that in meantime, I lost a grandmother.
She died, peacefully, in 1914-- '40.
That was my father's mother.
She was nearly 100 years old then.
And then a little bit later, after all the family dispersed everywhere, my grandfather, my mother's father, died peacefully.
And he was buried.
And then my-- onto my grandmother, from my mother's mother.
This was alive.
And I took her in, because she was all alone.
So I took her to-- just to our-- those little two rooms.
When my mother came, so we were too crowded.
So she found a room where she put my grandmother and my mother-in-law, my husband's mother, together, in the one room.
So that-- now it is '41, and we are in our flat.
I must tell that Przemysl was quiet till a whole year.
It means the first day we had to put on white band on our arm with a Magen David.
But we lived in our flat.
We had to observe the curfew.
We were not allowed to walk on the footpaths, for example.
We had to go out, always, in a group.
And-- But it was still-- we had-- look, we had jewelry, which we did not sell.
But we still had things which we could exchange with the peasant.
The peasant came to the city-- to the town with some food, meat or butter or something.
But they would not take any money, because there was no money.
There were rubles, which were not worth much.
You had to exchange.
So they got sheets, or bedding, or suits, and you know, all those things.
So you have to get up at five, six o'clock in the morning to catch a peasant woman in the street and to bargain with her for whatever she brought.
But it was still-- in comparison what came later, it was still a life which you could live.
But when the German comes all the jobs were finished.
So we still had to exchange.
Luckily, we still have what to exchange with those peasants.
And then, you know, my father started to do some business, all the-- on the black.
Well, of course.
We were doing some businesses, buying saccharin and selling materials or something like that, you see?
If you want an example, I will tell you.
One day, I came home.
I had a loose coat on.
And under there-- you see, I had the idea, when my parents came back from Lviv, to make a belt.
I had a big belt on which was stuffed with the whole jewelry of the whole family, jewelry.
I was very thin, so whatever I had on, nobody saw it anyway.
So I had this coat on, and I had two pieces of material for men's suits around me.
When I arrived home, I saw a policeman standing in the kitchen, my mother, pale, and the Germans sitting in the room.
And the German and then the one policeman in the other room.
They-- Somebody pointed them that the rich Jews came back.
So they come to-- to look for the-- for the richness.
And then I walk in with all of those things on me.
[LAUGHS]
And the German-- I had two beautiful photographs on-- this on-- of me, on the wall, one in a bathing suit and one in a white linen suit.
And he was sitting there with those two photographs, looking at them, the German.
And when I came in, he asked me, that's you? 'Yes.' And we started to talk.
He wondered how I come to have such a perfect German name, you know?
And then the policeman told-- told him that he wants to my personal-- no.
Search?
Search on me.
So I knew that we are all-- all gone then.
So I said, I just looked at this German and he told me, that's not done.
If you wanted to make a personal search, you should have brought a woman and not a man.
And do you know, he agreed with me.
And he told me, all right, and you are right.
And they left.
No.
They did not come back with a woman.
[LAUGHS] In the meantime, I would have disposed of all the things.
But anyway, that was one of the very many tight spots which I found myself.
Then in '42-- in July, '42, the Gestapo took over Przemysl.
And our peaceful-- relatively peaceful life finished.
Look, we knew what is happening all over Poland, somehow.
I don't know, because the post did not work.
The German could not understand.
They asked us always, how do you know what is happening everywhere?
We knew, for example, at the beginning, when they opened Belzec.
You heard about Belzec, where they started to-- they did not have yet the sophisticated way of disposing of the Jews.
They just burned them.
It we knew about other ghettos.
And of course-- but what happened in other countries, we did not know.
We-- afterwards, we knew a little bit about how the war is turning.
But we did not have any radios at this time, during the Russian time we had.
And that is why we expected the war to start, because we heard Churchill to-- to announce that if they don't start in June, it will be too late this year, because of the winter and of this.
But anyway, somehow, the-- you know, the-- how you call, the-- the news were traveling from one town.
Perhaps the Polish people were bringing it or somehow we knew what's happening everywhere else.
So when the Gestapo walked in, in '42, they ordered us, at once, out of our homes into the ghetto.
At the moment, it was quite a big part of the town, behind the railway line, which ran in the middle of the town.
So they put us behind the railway line.
And the only approach was a small bridge over the railway line.
Who told you that you had to go to the ghetto?
How were you informed?
Oh, it was announced.
First of all, we had this Jewish committee.
Yes?
And that was announced.
I can't remember how that was-- they-- they were announcing, every day, something else.
They-- anyway, the command came from the Gestapo headquarters that we have to move.
So we got a flat in the house right over the bridge.
It was, again, on the edge of the new-- the ghetto.
But this time we did not have the flat for our [? house, ?] but there was another couple living with us we had to share with.
Who was 'us,' just you and your husband?
No.
My parents, my husband and I, and this couple.
It was quite a big flat.
It was quite all right.
Well, we did not take any furniture, as you might see.
First of all, there was no means of carrying.
But the flat was furnished.
We took our personal things, our clothing.
Our-- we took-- for example, we had some silver left, the candelabras and things like that.
So we were-- we took what we could carry.
Oh, we-- it did not-- they did not give us long, only four hours, to do that.
But we made a few trips.
That day-- you see, I must tell-- tell you that my husband and my father worked for the Wehrmacht.
You know, Wehrmacht is the army.
They had their barracks.
They got a number of Jews, I think over 1,000 or more Jews, to work for them.
And that was in the morning.
They went to work.
We stayed at home.
Then--
Of course.
They were forced labor.
It-- So a week or two weeks after we moved to the ghetto, the Gestapo wants to walk in-- into the ghetto.
You know what for.
But then I saw a sight that I think it is unique in the history of this war.
The Wehrmacht, the army, occupied the bridge with machine guns against the Gestapo.
And they did not allow them to walk in to the ghetto.
That was Wehrmacht.
They kept all their workers, the Jewish workers, in their barracks.
My husband and my father did not come home, you see?
Then they kept them-- whenever it was a round-up, they kept their workers in the barracks.
They didn't let them to the ghetto.
Not to lose them, you see?
Or perhaps the commandant was a human being, you know?
I don't know.
But it was a known fact.
It was-- I was standing there at the window and saw it.
And I could not believe it.
You see?
But-- What--
That-- that day, the Gestapo left.
Well, just whatever they were told to do.
If I-- my husband was, I think, was a bricklayer or a plumber or something, which is very important.
Because the bricklayer, he played a-- a very important role in my husband's life.
You know?
He saved him.
And whatever they needed, it was needed to be done, manual work.
All only labor.
Only labor.
And-- but that day, as I told you, the Gestapo retreated and left.
But it did not last long.
Probably they got from Berlin, this--
This [INAUDIBLE]-- new commands, new-- and [? the ?] few days later, the Gestapo walked in.
By then, as I told you, we had some papers, my mother and I, stating that our husbands are working for the Wehrmacht.
And as we were the first flat and the first-- this-- the first house in the ghetto, that was the-- they come first to us.
And SS men, it was a group of soldiers and Ukrainian helpers.
He looked at our passes, which he accepted.
And they took away the other couple.
And then after a few minutes, we could hear from another you know, yards which where around us, and that the SS men was screaming, 'Everybody out!
Only those who have stamps from the Gestapo!'
So I was sure-- my mother looked at me.
I looked at my mother.
You see, we did not have stamps of the Gestapo.
But anyway, a few minutes later, a knock on the door.
So I was sure that he come back for us.
He come back.
But he asked me to come out.
So I told him, look, I'm going only to take my coat, because I had the white band on the coat.
And he tells me, 'You don't need it.' So I thought to myself, he's going to shoot me on the spot.
No, he wanted me as a guide to all the other flats in the room-- in the house.
And I did not know those people because we all moved in the-- in a matter of four hours, and they were strangers to me.
They broke into one flat.
I never saw in my life a fury like that, how they broke down the-- no-- the door, and the way that they started to demolish the flat.
At this moment, he turned around, the German.
And I must have been-- I don't know how I looked.
Because he stopped suddenly.
And he told me, you can go home.
I don't know.
Because nobody else was left on the first floor.
Only it was me and-- and my mother, you know?
So he just knocked at the door.
And as I would not let my mother to go close, I-- I-- I am telling you, I was only the one in the front.
So I went, so he took me.
And I could not [INAUDIBLE].

Later on I heard that when they rounded up all the Jews-- there were thousands and thousands on the square-- a man, a doctor, not even from Przemysl-- because they brought, in meantime, all the Jews from the little small town villages.
So the number of Jews in Przemysl swelled to 40,000.
Apparently this man, the doctor, spit on a German.
They shot him on the spot.
And then they went to-- back to his flat with a vengeance.
You know?
I did not know-- at the moment, I did not know why they are behaving like that.
But afterwards, I was told-- told that story.
Anyway, that day, this passed.
And we were told to move again, to a smaller area.
That was-- you see, they took many thousands there to die.
So we were left, I don't know how many.
But we were squeezed in the-- then--
Yes, of course, back into the ghetto, but into the smaller area.
Where we were, it was then out of the ghetto.
You see?
They squeezed us.
So we went in-- somehow my mother found people.
The new-- they were living in a house in the new ghetto.
But again, it was on the edge, right on the edge.
The house was overlooking the-- the road which did not belong to the ghetto anymore.
But at that time, they decreed that all those Wehrmacht passes for the women are not valid anymore.
Only the stamps, Gestapo stamps.
Luck was with us.
My husband got, without-- really without even trying-- he would not even know where to go-- he got two passes for me.
One with a photo, one without a photo, with a Gestapo stamp.
So I took my mother.
I gave her my pass.
And I had a friend who lived on the other side of the Ghetto, on the other-- quite a few streets from me.
And I took my mother there.
And my friend agreed to-- to keep her over there with my pass.
And I was praying only that somebody who knows her would not recognize that her name, that it's not on the pass.
You know?
But that was in relative safety.
A few days later, they walk-- the German are walking in again.
They got us all out.
And of course, I had the pass.
But they looked at the passes, everybody.
And suddenly the German saw me.
That was the-- the same who was the first day, in our house.
He saw me.
And he told the others, 'She is all right.
Leave her alone.'
You see?
But I was all right anyway, because I had the pass.
But he did not even look at the pass.
[? 'She is ?] all right.
[INAUDIBLE].
When I took my mother-- I wanted to tell you the-- this story.
When I took my mother to the other side of the ghetto, when I go-- went-- come back, I saw a sight.
It was a little square.
And on this square, there were people, old people, old Jews, kneeling with their face to the wall.
And a row of German was standing and shooting them, one by one.
I ran home.
I was absolutely shaken.
You know? 'Home,' what I called home, to this house.
But then I saw something which was even more terrible.
I was sitting at the window, and suddenly I saw-- I want to tell you this story, because it will be afterwards-- Well, then we'll hold it for a minute, change tapes.
Thank you.
Good.
